takeaway: A database is an organized collection of data, and the DBMS is the software that manages it: 'a database is an organized collection of data... based on the use of a database management system (DBMS), the software that interacts with end users, applications, and the database itself to capture and analyze the data.'

The relational model, introduced by Edgar Codd in 1969, lets you query data declaratively without knowing how it is stored: it is 'an approach to managing data using a structure and language consistent with first-order predicate logic.'

A relational database organizes data into tables linked by keys: 'A relational model organizes data into one or more tables (or "relations") of columns and rows, with a unique key identifying each row.'
A table holds related records in rows and columns: 'a table is a set of data elements (values) using a model of vertical columns and horizontal rows.'
SQL is the standard language for querying relational data; it 'introduced the concept of accessing many records with one single command,' a leap over earlier record-at-a-time APIs.
The database, not you, decides how to run a query efficiently: 'The purpose of query optimization, which is an automated process, is to find the way to process a given query in minimum time.'

A schema is the blueprint of the data: 'the schema defines the tables, fields, relationships, views, indexes, packages, procedures, functions, queues, triggers, types, sequences, materialized views, synonyms' and more.
A primary key is the column(s) that uniquely identifies a row: 'a designated set of attributes (column(s)) that can reliably identify and distinguish between each individual record in a table.'
A foreign key wires one table to another: it is 'a set of attributes that references a candidate key' — usually the primary key of a related table.
Normalization splits data to avoid redundancy and update bugs: a key objective is to 'free the collection of relations from undesirable insertion, update and deletion dependencies.'
An index is the trade that makes reads fast and writes a little slower: it 'improves the speed of data retrieval operations on a database table at the cost of additional writes and storage space.'
A transaction is an all-or-nothing unit of work: it must be 'atomic (it must either be complete in its entirety or have no effect whatsoever).'
ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) is the reliability contract for transactions: each one 'is treated as a single "unit", which either succeeds completely or fails completely.'
ACID is no longer relational-only: 'While early NoSQL databases lacked ACID compliance, modern systems like MongoDB provide full support for ACID transactions at scale.'
NoSQL drops fixed tables for flexible formats and 'scales easily to manage large, often unstructured datasets,' using key-value pairs, documents, and graphs instead of rows and columns.

Document stores keep each record as a self-contained unit: 'documents as self-contained units that encapsulate and encode data in a standardized format' such as JSON, so different records can have different shapes.

A key-value store is the simplest NoSQL shape: it treats 'the value as opaque to the database itself, and typically support[s] only simple operations such as storing, retrieving, updating, and deleting a value by its key.'

Replication keeps copies of the data on multiple servers for availability: 'In single-leader (also called primary/replica) replication, one database instance is designated as the leader (primary), which handles all write operations.'
Sharding splits the rows themselves across servers to scale out: 'Each shard may be held on a separate database server instance in order to spread [load] across multiple servers.'
The CAP theorem names the unavoidable trade-off in distributed databases: 'if there is a network partition, one has to choose between consistency or availability.'

Eventual consistency trades instant agreement for availability: replicas may briefly disagree, but 'a system that has achieved eventual consistency is said to have converged, or achieved replica convergence.'
Workload shape decides the database: OLTP handles many small live reads/writes, while 'OLAP is generally optimized for read-only operations' for analytics — the same data often lives in both.
